commit.message: Use the same type for the global variable and the initializing function
Use `ptrdiff_t` instead of `int64_t` for result of pointer subtraction

Address PR comments

1. Removed magic statics, since the function is called only once,
   especially given the loader lock used by Windows.

2. Used `adrp` + `ldr` to avoid the extra memory access to the literal
   pool.

Use static initializer for global offset

The `_tls_index` variable is unlike other C++ global variables, in that
even though the C++ standard does not guarantee relative ordering
between initialization of global variables in different translation
units, the PE loader in Windows ensures that `_tls_index` is initialized
before the C runtime code that performs the dynamic initialization of
any global variables.  So we can rest assured that referencing
`_tls_index` is our initialization function will not break the code.

Move offset initialization out of `cache_global_variables()`

The offset initialization now lives in the
`initialize_thr_current_tls_offset()` method of the `JavaThread` class,
since `_thr_current` is a private member of `Thread`, which is the base
class of `JavaThread`.  The `Assembler()` constructor calls the
`JavaThread` method via a forward-declared function to avoid having to
include all of runtime/javaThread.hpp in assembler_aarch64.hpp.
Use a static initializer for setting the offset

Implement `aarch64_get_thread_helper()` for Windows AArch64

Prior to this patch, on Windows/AArch64, `aarch64_get_thread_helper()`
resolved to `Thread::current()`, which, although correct, is suboptimal,
since it requires all registers from `x0` through `x17` to be saved by
the caller.  Linux/AArch64 has a hand-written assembly implementation
that clobbers just `x0` and `x1`, making it more efficient.  This patch
implements a similar hand-written assembly implementation for Windows.

A few caveats regarding how Windows handles thread-local storage causes
the implementation to be substantially different compared to one for
Linux, so some background information is perhaps necessary.  Windows
stores the pointer to the Thread Environment Block in the `x18`
register.   Offset 0x58 from this address contains the pointer to an
array of pointers to thread-local storages, one for each DLL or EXE that
requires a thread-local storage.  The C runtime library (MSVCRT)
combined with the program loader together ensure that the `_tls_index`
global variable (specific for each DLL or EXE) contains the index
(again, specific to the DLL or EXE that is accessing the variable)
within the array.  Since pointers are 8-bytes long, by derferencing the
address `*(x18 + 0x58) + (_tls_index * 8)`, we get the base address of
the thread-local storage.

To do the same thing that `Thread::current()` does, which is to fetch
`Thread::_thr_current`, we need to know the correct offset to
`_thr_current` in the thread-local storage.  We compute this offset in
C++ in `JavaThread::cache_global_variables()` and store in a global
variable.  When the hand-written assembly code is invoked, it computes
the address of the thread-local storage as explained in the previous
paragraph, before adding the C++-computed offset to get to
`_thr_current`.  The C++ code uses the `NtCurrentTeb()` function instead
of reading the `x18` register, primarily because MSVC doesn't permit
inline assembly, in addition to easier readability.
